text: ENNISMAIN, an insular parish in co. Galway, the central one of the three principal islands of the Arran archipelago, lying across the mouth of Galway bay. Area 2,252 acres. The highest ground has an alt. of 259 ft. Pop. in 1831, 424; in 1841, 473.
ENNISMORE, a township of Upper Canada, in the Colborne district, bordered on the NE and S by the chain of lakes which intersects the locality.
ENNISNAG, a parish 5 m. S of Kilkenny, in co. Kilkenny. Area 1,502 acres. Pop. in 1831, 550; river, and has an extreme elevation of 236 ft.
ENNISTRAHUL, a small island in the p. of Cloncha, co. Donegal. It lies 44 m. N of the nearest part of the mainland, and 7 m. E by N of Malin- Head. A revolving lighthouse is situated on the island.
ENNISTYMON, a small town in the p. of Kilmanaheen, co. Clare, on the Ennistymon river, <math>2\frac{1}{4}</math> m. E of the head of Liscanor-bay. Pop. in 1841, 2,089.
ENNISVILLE, a village of Barre township, Huntingdon co., in the state of Pennsylvania, U. S., on Sandingstone creek.

ENNOREE, a river of South Carolina, U. S., which takes its rise in Greenville co., 12 m. NW of the town of that name; separates the counties of Spartanberg and Union from those of Laurens and Newberry; and after a course in a generally SE direction of 75 m., unites with Broad river 40 m. NW of Columbia.
ENO, a town of Russia in Europe, in the grand duchy of Finland, gov. and 90 m. E of Kuopio, on Lake Pielis-Jarvi.
ENOCH, a township of Monroe co., in the state of Ohio, U.S., 105 m. E of Columbus. Pop. 1,134.
ENODER (Saint), a parish of Cornwall, 2½ m. NE of St. Michael. Area 4,050 acres. Pop. 1,127.
ENOGAT (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Ille-et-Vilaine, cant. of Pleurtuit. Pop. 1,818.

ENONTEKIS, a village of Russia in Europe, in the grand duchy of Finland, laen and 250 m. NNW of Uleaborg, near the shore and on the l. or E bank of the Muonio, in N lat. 68° 30', and at an alt. of 1,555 ft. above sea-level. A large fair is held here in the month of February, at which the merchants of Tornea exchange flour, salt, tobacco, coarse and fine cloths, hides, hemp, cordage, spoons, guns, axes, and hardware, for rein-deer skins, fox, hare, otter, beaver, squirrel, and ermine skins, dried pike, dried salmon, cod and stock fish, and butter. The sun is visible above the horizon here at midnight, during seven weeks of each year. The mean temp. is only 26°.85; but the mean temp. of the month of July is nearly 60°. A little barley is grown. The sunny season begins in May; and the * harvest is got in before the end of August, or even earlier. The most prolific return never exceeds sixfold. See on the subject of the climate of E. the 18th chapter of Prof. Thomson's Travels in Sweden. -The parish of E. has an area of 840 sq. m. Pop. 800, chiefly Laplanders, who employ themselves in fishing and the rearing of cattle.

ENORE, or ENUR, a village of Hindostan, in the Carnatic, situated on a flat sandy tract, on the bank of a salt lake, 8 m. N of Madras. It consists of an assemblage of native huts, about 100 in number, and 2 or 3 European houses. The lake abounds with excellent fish, and affords extensive supplies to the market of Madras.

ENOS, or Ænos, a seaport of Turkey in Europe, prov. of Romelia, sanjak and 37 m. NW of Gallipoli, and 70 m. SSW of Adrianople, at the W extremity of the peninsula which forms the SW side of the peninsula of the same name, and opposite the mouth of the Maritza, in N lat. 40° 40′ 30" and E long. 26° 3'. Pop. about 7,000. It has a commodious harbour, which is the actual port of Adrianople; but its entrance is obstructed by sand banks, and now scarcely admits the small craft from Smyrna. It enjoys, however, an active trade in wool, camel's and horse hair, cotton, leather, saffron, silk, wax, and copper. The Maritza has its banks lined with noble forests of oak and elm all the way from E. to Adrianople, and is navigable after the autumnal rains until the May following.—The gulf of E. runs ENE and is 14 m. in length, and 5 m. in average breadth. Its entrance does not exceed 2½ m. in breadth.
ENOSBURG, a township of Franklin co., in the state of Vermont, U. S., 56 m. NW of Montpelier, watered by Missisque river and its branches. Pop. in 1840, 2,022.
ENOSKOI, a village of Turkey in Europe, in Bulgaria, sanjak of Silistria, to the E of Shumla.

ENRAGE (CAPE), a headland of the island of Martinique, on the W coast, 6 m. NW of Port Royal, in N lat. 14° 40', and W long. 61° 7'. ENRIQUELLO, HONRIQUELLO, OF SALT LAKE, a sheet of water in the island of Hayti, in the dep. of Ozama, 36 m. E of Port-au-Prince, and to the W of the great plain of Neybe. It is about 29 m. in length from E to W, and 9 m. in its greatest breadth, and is very deep. Near its centre is the island of Cabrits. It receives the Damas, Berbicie, and several other rivers. It is 21 m. from and has no apparent communication with the sea; but its waters are salt and rise and fall with the tides. ENS, ENNS, or ENZ, a river of Austria, which takes its rise in the circle of Salzburg, on the N side of the Radstathr-Tauern, 12 m. S of Radstadt; runs N past that town, and then bending ENE, enters the duchy of Styria; at Hieflau it directs its course to the N, passes into the archduchy of Austria, separates the provinces of Upper and Lower Austria, the waters Steyer and Ens, and after a total course of 162 m. discharges itself into the Danube, opposite Maulhausen, and 2 m. below the town of Ens. Its principal affluents are the Salza-Styrian on the r., and on the l. the Steyer. It is navigable to Riefling, but can be ascended by small boats as far as Steyer. ENS, ENNS, or EMS, a fortified town of the archduchy of Austria, prov. of Upper Austria, circle of Traun, 14 m. N of Steyer, and 90 m. W of Vienna, on the l. bank of the river of the same name, about 2 m. SSW of its confluence with the Danube. Pop. 3,400. It is well built, and contains a castle, an ancient archducal edifice, an arsenal, and 3 churches. It has manufactories of iron, steel, linen, calico, and ribbon, and although greatly declined in importance, still possesses a considerable trade. E. is one of the oldest towns in Austria, and is supposed to occupy AND THE PROPERTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PAR ENT the site of the ancient Anisia, Anasum, or Ensium Civitas of the Romans. It was rebuilt in 900 by the Bavarians to protect their frontier from the aggressions of the Avares. The treaty of peace entered into in 1336 between Austria and Bohemia took place here; and here Napoleon established his head-quarters in 1809. In the vicinity is the castle of Enseck.
ENS (UPPER and LOWER). See AUSTRIA (ARCHDUCHY OF).
ENSALA, a Tuarick town of Sudan, the most eastern in the prov. of Tuat, in N lat. 27° 11', E long. 2° 15′, 35 days from Timbuctu.
ENSAY, one of the Hebrides, in the p. of Harris, and co. of Inverness. It is 2 m. long, and 1 m. in breadth, and is well cultivated.
ENSCHEDE, a town of Holland, in the prov. of Overyssel, 15 m. SE of Almelo, near the source of one of the affluents of the Regge. Pop. 5,000. It has manufactories of linen and cotton fabrics.

ENSDORF, a town of Bavaria, in the circle of Regen, presidial and 10 m. SE of Amberg.—Also a village of Austria, in the prov. of Lower Austria, in the circle of Wiener Walde, on the r. bank of the Ens.
ENSEIDLIN. See EINSIEDELN.
ENSEN, a village of Prussia, in the prov. of the. Rhine, regency of Cologne, on the Rhine, SE of Deutz. Pop. 387.
ENSENADA, a town of La Plata, in the dep. and 30 m. SE of Buenos-Ayres, on the S side of the bay of Ensenada-de-Barragan.
ENSENADA-DE-BARRAGAN, a bay on the W side of the estuary of the Rio-de-la-Plata, in the dep. and 30 m. SE of Buenos-Ayres, enclosed on the E by Point Santiago.
ENSHAM, a parish and village of Oxfordshire, on the Isis, which is here crossed by two handsome stone bridges, 5 m. ESE of Witney. Area of p., 5,060 acres. Pop. 1,893.
ENSHEIM, a village of the grand duchy of Hessen, N of Alzey. Pop. 479.

ENSISHEIM, or Ensen, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Haut-Rhin, arrond. of Colmar.—The cant. comprises 17 com. Pop. in 1831, 14,858; in 1841, 17,193.—The town is situated in a fertile plain, on the r. bank of the Ill. Pop. in 1841, 3,747. It is surrounded by walls and ditches, and is well built. The town-house is a handsome Gothic structure. The ancient Jesuit college, erected in 1614 by the emperor Maximilian, has been converted into a house-of-detention. It has manufactories of calico and straw hats, and a cotton spinning-mill.—This town is one of the most ancient in the dep. It was formerly capital of Austrian Alsace. During the Thirty years' war it sustained several sieges. In 1634 it was taken by the Swedes, in 1637 by the French, and in 1638 by Charles of Lorrain. Its cession to France took place at the peace of Munster.
ENSIVAL, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Liége, cant. and 10 m. N of Spa, on the Vesdre. Pop. in 1842, 2,968. It has extensive manufactories of cloth, and contains quarries of freestone.
ENSOKO, a town of Upper Guinea, in the kingdom of Dagumba, 30 m. ENE of Yandy.
ENSON. See SALT and ENSON.
ENSTONE, a parish of Oxfordshire. Area 4,850 acres. Pop. 1,121. It comprises the hamlets of Church-Enstone and Neat-Enstone, containing respectively 237 and 378 inhabitants.
ENSUTA, a town of Upper Guinea, in the kingdom of Ashantee, 50 m. WNW of Komassi.

ENTCHETCAB, or Inchetcaub, a town of Abyssinia, in the prov. of Samen, in about 13° 15' N lat., 35 m. NE of Donga, in an undulating plain at an alt. of about 10,000 ft. above sea-level, but CONTRACTOR OF THE PROPERTY OF THE STATE OF THE STATE OF THE STATE OF THE STATE OF THE STATE OF THE STATE OF THE STATE OF THE STATE OF THE STATE OF THE STATE OF THE STATE OF THE STATE OF THE STATE OF THE STATE OF THE STATE sloping rapidly on the W to the Shoada or valley of the Bellegas, which is about 4,000 ft. lower.
ENTER, a village of Holland, in the prov. of Overyssel, cant. of Goor, 6 m. SW of Almelo. Pop. 1,600.
ENTERPRISE (FORT), a settlement in British North America, in the district of the Great Slave lake, on the W side of Winter lake, in N lat. 64° 20', and W long. 113° 2'.
ENTFELDEN, or ENDFELDEN, a circle and village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Argau, 4 m. S of Aarau, on the r. bank of the Suhren, and on the road from Zurich to Berne. Pop. 1,086.

ENTLIBUCH, an amt or bailiwick, parish and village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Luzern. The amt, consisting of the fine pastoral valley of the same name, comprises the whole of that portion of the valley of the Emmen which lies above Wollhausen. It contains an area of 220 sq. m., and is divided into the circles of Entlibuch, Escholzmatt, and Schüpfheim. Pop. in 1837, 18,065. "By the energy of their character," says Ebel, "they are among the most remarkable of the Alpine pop. of Switzerland."-The v., which is the chief place in the valley, is situated on the r. bank of the Emme, near the confluence of the Entle, at the W base of the Bramegg, and at an alt. of 2,400 ft. above sealevel, 13 m. WSW of Luzern. Pop. 2,741.

ENTRADAS, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Alemtejo, comarca and 16 m. NE of Ourique, on the l. bank of the Corbes. ENTRAGUES, ANTRAGUE, or ENTRAQUE, a commune and village of Sardinia, in Piedmont, in the prov. and 13 m. SSW of Coni, on an affluent of the Gesso. Pop. 3,147. In the vicinity is an iron mine.

ENTRAIGUES, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Isere, cant. of Valbonnais, on the Bonne, 26 m. SE of Grenoble. Pop. 557.—Also a commune in the dep. of Puy-de-Dome, cant. of Ennezat. Pop. 1,114.—Also a commune in the dep. of Vaucluse, cant. and 8 m. SW of Carpentras. Pop. 1,406. It has a paper-mill.
ENTRAINS, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of the Nievre, cant. of Varzy, 12 m. W of Clamecy, in a marshy locality. Pop. 2,163.
ENTRAMBAS-AGUAS, a judicial partido and town of Spain, in the prov. of Santander. The partido comprises 72 pueblos or towns. The town is old, but contains little worthy of notice. Pop. 1,635.
ENTRAMES, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Mayenne, cant. and 6 m. SSE of Laval. Pop. 1,237. It has an abbey, and possesses an extensive paper manufactory.
ENTRANCE ISLAND, an islet lying a 1 m. outside of Brecknock harbour, on the N coast of Australia, in S lat. 15° 27', E long. 124° 30'.
ENTRATICO, a village of Venetian Lombardy, in the prov. and 12 m. E of Bergamo, in the Val. Cavallina, on the l. bank of the Chario. Pop. 450. In the environs is a mine of marble.
ENTRECASTEAUX, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Var, cant. of Cotignac. on the Bresque, 14 m. NE of Brignoles. Pop. 2,200.

ENTRECASTEAUX (CHANNEL D'), a strait on the SE coast of Van Diemen's Land, which has its S entrance between Tasman's head and South cape, and runs NNE along the W side of Brune island, from Whale-head on the SSW, to the estuary of the Derwent, where it terminates between Cape Pierson and Cape Sortie or Get-out. It is about 40 m. in length; and varies from 2 to 12 m. in breadth; and its sinuous coasts present several extensive estuaries and bays, which afford good anchorage. Of these, the principal on the W side of the channel are Recherche bay and Port Arthur, CONTENTAL CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CON ENT Muscle bay or South Port, Esperance bay or Adamson's harbour, Huon river, an extensive estuary running along the W confines of Hobart-Town district, and North-west bay; and, indenting the shore of Brune island, are Great Cove or Taylor's bay, Little Cove, and Isthmus bay. Its depth varies from 6 to 15 fath.; and numerous islands stud its surface, but they are generally small. ENTRECASTEAUX (D') ISLANDS, a group of islands in the S. Pacific, in the Louisiade archipelago, ESE of New Guinea. They were discovered by Bougainville in 1768; and in 1793 were visited by the navigator from whom they take their name.
ENTRECASTEAUX (Point D'), a headland of S. W. Australia, in the county of Lanark, in S lat. 34° 52′, and E long. 116° 1′.
ENTRECHAUX, a commune of France, in the dep. of Vaucluse, cant. of Malancène. Pop. 1,110.
ENTRE-DEUX-GUYERS, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Isère, cant. of Saint-Laurent-du- Pont. Pop. 1,739. It has a custom-house.

ENTRE-DEUX-MERS, a district of France, in the dep. of the Gironde, noted for its wine. It is situated between the Dordogne and Garonne, and comprises the cantons of Branne, Pujols, Pellegre, and partly those of Sauveterre and Creon. Besides its produce for home-consumption and for the Paris market, it exports large quantities of wine to Great Britain and to the ports of the Baltic.

ENTRE DOURO-E-MINHO, the most northern prov. of Portugal, lying, as its name imports, between the Douro and the Minho rivers; bounded on the W by the Atlantic; on the N by the Spanish prov. of Galicia, from which it is separated by the Minho; and on the S by the Douro which separates it from Beira. Its extent from S to N is 60 m.; its breadth from E to W less than 40 m. It is a high table-land, intersected by several mountain-ridges running from NE to SW. The climate is healthy, and the inhabitants live in greater comfort than in any other part of Portugal. The highest grounds are generally barren, but the middle tract is covered with plantations or rich pasturage; while the valleys are productive in maize, wine, oil, fruit, and, to a certain degree, in corn and flax. The richest part of the whole is that adjacent to the Douro, which produces the well-known port wine. The superior fertility of this prov. is owing to its being well-watered, partly by natural streams, and partly from a vast number of wells obtained by digging. There are several good harbours on the coast; also considerable fisheries. The cap. is Braga; but the most important town is Oporto.—Under recent arrangements this prov. has been divided into the two provs. of Minho and Douro; the former containing 171 sq. leagues of 20 to a degree, with a pop. in 1841, of 466,720; and the latter 324 sq. leagues, with a pop. of 839,908.
ENTREMONT-LE-VIEUX, a village of Sardinia, in Savoy, 9 m. S of Chambery, on a torrent named Coson, an affluent of the Guier. Pop. 1,450. See also Saint-Pierre-D'Entremont.
ENTRENA, a town of Spain, in Old Castile, in the prov. and partido and 6 m. SW of Logrono.

ENTRE-RIOS, a prov. of La Plata, comprised— as its name implies-between two great rivers, the Uruguay and Parana, and by them respectively separated from Banda-Oriental-del-Uruguay on the E; from Buenos Ayres on the S; and on the W from the prov. of Santa Fé. On the N it is bounded by the provs. of Missiones and Corrientes. Its area is estimated at 32,000 sq. m.; and its pop. at 150,000. It is intersected longitudinally by ranges of hills, from which the Noboya, Gualeguay, Gualeguay-chu, and numerous smaller streams descend to the great rivers which form its borders. The S part presents an extensive delta formed by the Parana, and is annually inundated; and, throughout the entire extent of the prov., low swampy ground is to be met with, alternating with wide and luxuriant prairies. The soil is fertile, and the climate salubrious, but cultivation is still very limited and carelessly conducted. It is almost entirely occupied with estancias or cattle farms; and the principal productions of the locality, consisting in hides, horns, tallow, and jerked beef, are derived from the immense herds of cattle which are to be found in the prairies.—The prov. is subdivided by the river Gualeguay into 2 deps., known as the dep. of the Parana, and the dep. of the Uruguay. It formed part of the intendency of Buenos- Ayres till 1814.—The cap., Badaja, or Villa-del- Parana, is situated on the river from which it takes its name, opposite to Santa-Fé. Ybicuy in the S, and Concepcion-de-la-China in the E, are also considerable towns.

ENTREVAUX, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Basses-Alpes, arrond. of Castellane.—The cant. comprises 8 com. Pop. in 1831, 3,269; in 1841, 3,492.—The town, situated on a height near the l. bank of the Var, is 18 m. NE of Castellane. Pop. in 1841, 1,732. It is strongly fortified, and has a citadel, an ancient cathedral, an alms-house, and barracks.—This town is of great antiquity. Its fortifications were erected in 1690, and in 1704 it successfully defended itself against the king of Sardinia, and obliged him to raise the siege.
ENTREVERNE, a village of Sardinia, in Savoy, in the prov. of Genevois, 10 m. S of Annecy. In the vicinity are mines of coal.
ENTRINGEN, a town of Wurtemberg, in the circle of the Schwarzwald, bail. and 7 m. SE of Herrenberg. Pop. 1,224. It has manufactories of linen, calico, and kirschwasser.

ENTRY ISLAND, an island in the gulf of St. Lawrence, in the Magdalene group, in N lat. 47° 15', and W long. 61° 30'. It comprises an area of nearly 100 sq. arpents, and is inhabited by a few fishermen.

ENTRY ISLAND, or KAPITI, an island of New Zealand, in Cook's strait, near the SW extremity of New Ulster, in S lat. 40° 50′, and E long. 174° 55′. It is of an irregular oval outline, 5 m. in length from NE to SW, and averages 1 m. in breadth. It consists of a ridge of hills, whose highest summit rises 600 ft. above sea-level. It belongs to the transition formation, and is covered with a vigorous vegetation. Potatoes, cabbage, turnips, and Indian corn thrive here; but its chief importance is as a shore-whaling station. Cattle thrive well upon the island.
ENTWISLE, a township of Lancashire. in the p. and 6 m. NNE of Bolton. Area 1,450 acres. Pop. 555.
ENUITAK, or ONARTOK, a village of Greenland, on the S coast, in the district of Julianas Haab.
ENVEIGHT, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Pyrénées-Orientales, cant. of Saillagousse. Pop. 435.
ENVENDOS, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Alemtejo, comarca and 23 m. NNW of Crato, on the r. bank of the Tagus. Pop. 1,640.

ENVERMEU, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Seine-Inferieure, arrond. of Dieppe. The cant. comprises 30 com. Pop. in 1831, 14,547; in 1841, 15,041.—The town is 10 m. E of Dieppe, and 36 m. NNE of Rouen. Pop. in 1846, 1,407. It has a brewery, an oil-mill, several bleacheries, and an extensive nursery-garden. A grain-market is held weekly, and a fair for cattle twice a-year.
ENVILLE, or Enfield, a parish of Staffordshire, ENZ 5<sup>3</sup> m. WNW of Stourbridge. Area 4,930 acres. Pop. 814.
ENXU, or Exu, a river of Brazil, which forms part of the boundary-line between the prov. of Ceara and Piauhi.
ENXARA-DOS-CAVALLEIROS, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Estremadura, comarca and 8 m. SE of Torres-Vedras.

ENY (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Manche, cant. of Carentan, 15 m. NW of Saint Lo. Pop. 1,772. ENYED (NAGY), STRASSBURG, EGIDSTADT, ENGETEN, or AJUD, a town of Transylvania, cap. of the gespanschaft of Unter-Alba, on a small river, near the r. bank of the Maros, and 18 m. NNE of Carlsburg. Pop., consisting of Hungarians, Saxons, Wallachians, and Armenians, 5,448. It has a large Protestant college, a Catholic and 2 Protestant churches, a convent, a library and museum, and a gymnasium; and possesses a manufactory of knapsacks. The college is chiefly supported by an annual revenue of £1,000, arising from funds still deposited in the bank of England, and originally contributed in England. It is attended by about 1,000 students, of whom about 300 are togati. It has 8 professors. This town belongs to the number of oppidum nobilium, or towns freed from the jurisdiction of the gsp., and governed by a local magistracy. The vine is extensively cultivated in the environs.
ENYICZKE, a town of Hungary, in the comitat of Abaujvar, 8 m. S of Kaschau. It has a castle, in which a consistory was held by the Jews in 1650.

ENZ, a river of Germany, which issues from the Poppel-see, in the Schwarzwald circle of Wurtemberg, 18 m. S of Wildbad; passes that town and Nuenbürg; enters Baden, and waters Pforzheim, Dürrmenz, and Vaihingen; then re-enters Wurtemberg near Enzberg, and, after a total course of 80 m., falls into the Neckar, on the l. bank, a little below Besigheim. Alt. at its source, 2,505 ft.; at Wildbad, 1,358 ft.; at Pforzheim, 1,007 ft.; at Vaihingen, 659 ft.; and at its embouchure, 580 ft. Its principal affluent is the Nagold, which it receives on the r. bank.

ENZBERG, a village of Wurtemberg, in the circle of the Neckar, bail. and 7 m. S of Maulbronn, on the Enz, on the confines of Baden. Pop. in 1840, 1,228. It has a paper-mill. ENZELLE, ENZELLI, INZELI, or ZINZILI, a town of Persia, in the prov. of Ghilan, 15 m. NNW of Reshd, at the extremity of a low narrow neck of land, which separates the lake or bay of the same name on the NW, from the Caspian sea, and on the W side of a navigable passage connecting the lake with the sea. Pop., chiefly Russian, about 1,000. It was greatly reduced in numbers by the plague in 1830-1. The houses, about 300 in number, are built of wood and roofed with reeds. In the centre of the town is a commodious and well-stocked bazaar. There are also several caravanserais, an arsenal, and a few detached towers for its defence. E. is the only Persian port on the Caspian. Its harbour is formed by an island which stretches along the S side of the W point, leaving a deep channel of about 150 yds. wide, in which vessels may ride close to shore. About 130 vessels, varying from 40 to 100 tons burden, are employed in trading betwixt E. and the Russian coasts of the Caspian. [Holmes.] The customs of E. yield about 6,000 tomauns, or £3,000 annually. The import trade consists chiefly in grain. The principal exports are silk, rice, dried fruits, cotton, and articles of local manufacture. Vessels of various sizes are built here.—The bay of E. is a large backwater, separated from the Caspian on the N by a low sandy tongue of land, varying from a few 100 yds. to a <math>\frac{1}{2}</math> m. in breadth, through which two openings have been forced by the sea,one at the town of the same name, 500 yds. in breadth and about 4 ft. in depth, -and the other 6 m. further SSE. The length of the lake, from E to W, is from 35 to 40 m., and its breadth from 12 to 15 m. [Fraser.] It varies in depth from 8 to 10 ft. Its shores are low and fringed with wood; and it is studded with numerous reed-covered islands. Its waters are fresh, being contributed by numerous rivers and rivulets, the principal of which are the Mushed- Rudbar, the Bulgur, the Siarvi, the Kiftarud, the Khallakagi, the Kumiskal, the Hindu-Kaleh, and the Ghulish-Kail. Among the multitudes of fish with which the lake swarms, are the sturgeon, the bream, the salmon, carp, and a fish called sefid-mahi, which is caught with a casting-net, and is largely consumed in the prov. Pheasants are abundant on the islands. The boats on the lake are about 25 ft. long, sharp at both ends, and flat-bottomed; and carry a square sail when there is little wind.

ENZERSDORF (GROSS), or STAEDTL-ENZERSDORF, a town of Austria, in the prov. of Lower Austria, circle of Mannhartsberg, on the l. bank of an arm of the Danube, 8 m. E of Vienna, and 13 m. SE of Korn-Neuburg, on the railway to Stockerau. Pop. 823. It is fortified, and has a military school. In 1809 it was bombarded and reduced to ashes by

ENZERSDORF (MARIA), a village of Austria, in the French. the prov. of Lower Austria, and SW of Vienna. Pop. 820. It has a Franciscan convent. In the environs are the fine modern castle of Liechtenstein and the remains of an ancient castle.
ENZESFELD, a town of Austria, in the prov. of Lower Austria, circle of Mannhartsberg, E of Korn- Neuberg.—Also a town in the circle of the Wiener- Walde, 10 m. NNE of Neustadt, and 23 m. SSW of

ENZWEIHINGEN, a town of Wurtemberg, in Vienna. the circle of the Neckar, bail. and 11 m. SE of Vaihingen, on the r. bank of the Enz, which is here crossed by a fine bridge, and at an alt. of 2,044 ft. above sea-level. Pop. 1,579. It has a paper-mill. EO, or MIRANDA, a river of Spain, in the prov. of Lugo, which rises in and runs NNE parallel to the range of mountains which forms the NE side of the bed of the Minho; directs its course thence to the borders of the prov. of Oviedo, and forms, for the distance of 15 m., the line of separation between the prov. After a total course of 42 m. it falls into the Atlantic about a mile NE of Ribadeo.

EOUA, or MIDDLEBURG, an island of the South Pacific, in the group of the Tonga or Friendly islands, in S lat. 21° 24', and W long. 174° 55'. It is about 30 m. in circumf., and is of coral formation, rising abruptly on the NE to the height of 600 ft. above sea-level. On the NW it is generally flat. The principal productions of its soil are fruit, sugar, and bananas. It possesses few quadrupeds. The inhabitants are of a dark copper colour, with black curly hair, and are handsome, brave, and intelligent, but treacherous. They use as arms massive clubs, lances, and bows of peculiar construction; and manufacture mats, various kinds of cloth, and long double canoes. E. was discovered by Tasman in 1643, and named by him Middleburg.
EOUAIGEE, an islet of the South Pacific, in the group of the Friendly islands, to the E of Tongatabu.
EOUSMIL, an insulated rock, about half-a-mile in circuit, lying on the W side of North Uist, noted
EPAGNE, or EPAIGNES, a commune of France, for its seal-fishery. in the dep. of the Eure, cant. of Cormeilles, 7 m. SSW of Point-Audemar. Pop. 2,317. EPF
EPARGNES, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Charente-Inferieure, cant. of Cozes. Pop. 1,525.
EPARRES (LES), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Isère, cant. of Bourgoin. Pop. 1,675.
EPE, a town of Holland, in the prov. of Gelderland, 25 m. N of Arnheim, on an affluent of the
EPEHY, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Grift. Pop. 4,205. Somme, cant. of Roisel, 12 m. NE of Peronne. It has manufactories of linen, damask, and of varieties of cotton fabrics.

EPENSE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Marne, cant. of Dommartin-sur-Yevre, 11 m. SW of Sainte-Menehoulde. Pop. 359. It has a manufactory of fine china-ware, and a considerable trade in dried pease.

EPERIES, or HEPERJES, a royal free town of Hungary, capital of the gespanschaft of Saros, on the l. bank of the Tarcza, 22 m. NNE of Kaschau, and 143 m. NE of Pest. Pop. in 1846, 8,600, chiefly Germans. This town, which is one of the best built in Upper Hungary, is of oblong form and has extensive suburbs. It is strongly fortified, and has a cathedral with a fine clock-tower, a Catholic and two Lutheran churches, a synagogue, a town-house, a Lutheran college, a Catholic gymnasium, and a printing establishment. It has extensive manufactories of linen, cloth, and other woollen fabrics; and an active trade in wine, grain, cattle, and articles of local manufacture. A well-frequented fair is held once a-year.—To the E of the town, at Kraszna- Voda, are the mineral springs and baths of Czeméthe.

EPERNAY, an arrondissement, canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Marne. -The arrond. comprises an area of 211,648 hect., and contains 9 cant., viz. Anglure, Avize, Dormans, Epernay, Esternay, Fère-Champenoise, Montmirail, Montmort, and Sezanne. Pop. in 1831, 83,278; in 1846, 91,387.—The cant. comprises 11 com. Pop. in 1831, 14,004; in 1841, 15,034.—The town is pleasantly situated in a fine valley near the l. bank of the Marne, which is here crossed by a handsome bridge, 17 m. S of Rheims, and 21 m. WSW of Chalons-sur-Marne. Pop. in 1789, 3,692; in 1821, 4,839; in 1831, 5,318; in 1841, 5,978; and in 1846, 5,926. It is well-built, and possesses a communal college, a theatre, a trades-house, and a printing-press. It has extensive manufactories of pottery, cloth, and other woollen fabrics, and leather, and is one of the principal entrepots for the wines of Champagne, which are here kept in vaults excavated in the sandstone rock on which the town is built. By means of a small port on the Marne, it carries on also an active trade in wood, millstones, bottles, utensils of various kinds used in the making of wine, corks, &c. Fairs are held several times a-year. This town was formerly a place of great strength, but its fortifications have been suffered to fall into ruins. It was burnt by Francis I., and in 1592 sustained a siege by Henry IV., in which Marshal Biron was killed.

EPERNON, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of the Eure-et-Loire, cant. of Maintenon, 21 m. NE of Chartres. Pop. in 1841, 1,553. The town, which is one of considerable antiquity, is situated on the brow of a hill, near the junction of three rivers, and has a fine castle and an hospital. It has manufactories of cloth and leather, and a considerable trade in grain and flour.
EPESSEZ (LES), a commune of France, in the dep. of Vendée, cant. of Les Herbiers, 34 m. NE of Bourbon-Vendée. Pop. 1,478. It has a paper-mill.
EPFENBACH, a town of Baden, in the circle of the Lower Rhine, N of Waibstadt, at an alt. of 410 ft. above sea-level. Pop. 1,220.
EPFENDORF, a village of Wurtemberg, in the circle of the Schwarzwald, on the Neckar, to the N | of Rottweil. Pop. 765.
EPFENHAUSEN, a village of Bavaria, in the circle of Upper Bavaria, on the Lech, N of Landsberg. Pop. 160.
EPFIG, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Bas-Rhin, cant. of Bar, 9 m. N of Schelestat. Pop. in 1841, 2,861. Potters' clay is found in the locality.

EPHESUS, anciently one of the most splendid cities of Asia Minor, and considered the metropolis of all Asia, but now represented by a miserable Turkish v. of some 40 scattered huts, the habitation of herdsmen and farmers, who live in low and mean huts, and are sheltered from the weather by large masses of ruinous walls. It was situated originally about 50 m. S of Smyrna, near the mouth and on the S side of the river Cayster, which flows into a bay of the Ægean sea; but it has been so often destroyed and rebuilt, that its precise situation cannot be ascertained. It was known in ancient times under various designations; and it continued to flourish under the Roman emperors. When Christianity, under the auspices of Constantine and Theodosius, became the established religion of the empire, numerous churches were erected here on the ruins of the pagan temples. Under the reign of Alexius, father of Anna Comnena, the Mahommedans made themselves masters of E. It was retaken from them by the Greeks in 1206; but was finally lost in 1283; and from the commencement of the 15th cent. has formed part of the Turkish dominions. Long before the capture of Constantinople by the Turks, and the extinction of the Greek empire, the ancient city had fallen into general decay; and a new town and citadel having been founded at Aiasaluk, or Ajasoluk, about 2 m. distant, the ancient city was soon totally abandoned. E. was considered the lumen Asiæ, -the chief ornament of Asia, and is mentioned by different historians as the greatest and most frequented emporium of that continent.—The great ornament of the city was the celebrated temple of Diana, which was built at the joint expense of all the states of Asia, and for its structure, size, and splendour of decoration, accounted one of the wonders of the world. It was 425 ft. long; 220 ft. broad; and was supported by 127 pillars, each 70 ft. high. This celebrated edifice was destroyed during the invasion of the Goths, A. D. 260; and so completely has the work of ruin been performed that its site now affords matter of conjecture and controversy to travellers. The port of E. is now choked up by a pestiferous morass; and the river winds through a muddy plain, amid lofty reeds, with a slow turbid stream. A part of the aqueduct which brought water into the city is still to be seen, extending from the hill of the fortress to the E. There are also two vast gateways of a theatre, and some walls of brick faced with marble slabs, supposed either to have formed a part of the temple of Diana, or of the church of St. John; and at Aiasaluk, about a m. to the NE, is a large portal wholly built with Roman tiles faced with polished marble. The mosque of the v. contains 4 colossal red granite columns, which, it is supposed, may have belonged to the great temple of Diana; but even this mosque is fast going to decay. See Alasaluk. The mountain precipices rising over E., -the Prion and the Caryssus,-shoot up in picturesque forms to the height of 700 or 800 ft., and their faces are perforated with quarries, whence doubtless was drawn much of the materials used in the construction of the ancient city.

EPHRAIM TOWN, a town of W. Africa, on the E bank of the Calabar river, 50 m. above its mouth. It is of considerable size, and contains a pop. of at least 6,000. The breadth of the river opposite the town is nearly that of the Thames at Waterloo bridge. - EPI It is full of alligators from 12 to 14 ft. long. The houses are of clay, and built in an irregular manner, with narrow roads between them. A considerable traffic is conducted with this town by French and English ships. The chief article of export is palm-oil.

EPHRATAH, a township of Fulton co., in the state of New York, U.S., 58 m. WNW of Albany, drained by Garoga and Zimmerman's creek, branches of the Mohawk river. It has an irregular surface, and its soil is a sandy loam. Pop. in 1840, 2,009.— Also a township of Lancaster co., in the state of Pennsylvania, 38 m. SE of Harrisburg, founded by a sect of German enthusiasts called Funkers. Pop. 2,083.

EPIDAUROS, a village of Greece, in the Morea, in the dio. of Argolis, 20 m. ENE of Nauplia, on the gulf of Ægina. It has a custom-house; and still possesses the ruins of the famous temple of Epidaurus. The first Greek congress was held here in 1821.

EPIERRE, a village of Sardinia, in Savoy, in the prov. of Maurienne, 6 m. S of Aiguebelle, near the r. bank of the Arc, at an alt. of 1,212 ft. above sealevel. Pop. 400. In the environs are several blastfurnaces and quarries of gypsum.

EPILA, a town of Spain, in Aragon, in the prov. and 27 m. W of Saragossa, partido of Almunia, on the l. bank of the Jalon. Pop. 3,152. It has a parish-church, a palace belonging to the count of Aranda, a convent, several hospitals, &c. Its industry consists chiefly in agriculture, and in the manufacture of a few varieties of cloth.

EPINAC, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of the Saone-et-Loire, arrond. of Autun.-The cant. comprises 11 com. Pop. in 1831, 7,818; in 1841, 9,431. The com. has extensive coal-mines. In 1839 they comprised an area of 7,099 hect. and produced 626,637 quintals. It is intersected to the extent of 21 m. by the railroad which runs from Epinac to Pont d'Ouche on the Burgundy canal. Pop. 2,803. The village, situated on an eminence, on which are the ruins of a castle, is 12 m. ENE of Autun, and 54 m. NNE of Maçon. It has extensive bottle-works, and manufactories of sugar from beet-root.

EPINAL, an arrondissement, canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Vosges.—The arrond. comprises an area of 147,048 hect., and contains 6 cant., viz.: Bains, Bruyères, Châtel-sur- Moselle, Epinal, Rambervillers, and Xertigny. Pop. in 1831, 91,578; in 1846, 99,356.—The cant. comprises 22 com. Pop. in 1831, 19,606; in 1841, 21,901. The town is situated at the foot of the Vosges, at an alt. of 1,034 ft. above sea-level, 43 m. SSE of Nancy, and 225 m. ESE of Paris, in N lat. 48° 10' 24", E long. 6° 26′ 33″. Pop. in 1789, 7,711; in 1821, 7,941: in 1831, 9,070; in 1841, 11,012. It is divided into two nearly equal parts by the Moselle, and has fine promenades running along the banks of the river. It is well-built, and has a college, a fine Gothic church, a museum, a public library of 17,000 vols., a theatre, academies of drawing and music, and 2 hospitals. It has extensive manufactories of cutlery, iron and copper ware, china, hosiery, paper, and oil; several tanneries and saw-mills, and 3 printing establishments. Its trade consists chiefly in grain, wine, horses, cattle, paper, and linen. Fairs are held twice a-month.—The foundation of this town is attributed to Theodoric d'Hamelan, bishop of Metz, in- 980. In 1466 it was bestowed upon the duke of Lorrain. Its fortifications were destroyed in 1670. On an eminence commanding the town are the ruins of a castle. Marble of various colours is quarried in the environs.
EPINAY-SUR-SEINE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Seine, cant. and 4 m. NNW of St. Denis, on the r. bank of the Seine. Pop. 870. It has some manufactories, and extensive market-gardens.
EPINAY-LE-COMTE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Orne, cant. of Passais. Pop. 1,189.
EPINE, a hamlet of France, in the dep. of the Nord, cant. and com. of Solre-le-Château. Pop. 2,274.
EPINETTE-CROUZET, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Hainault, cant. of Templeuve. Pop. 170.
EPINEU-LE-CHEVREUIL, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Sarthe, cant. of Loué. Pop. 1,043.
EPINEUIL, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Cher, cant. of Saulzais-le-Potier. Pop. 1,074.— Also a commune in the dep. of the Yonne, cant. of Tonnerre. Pop. 618. It is noted for its wines.
EPINIAC, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Ille-et-Vilaine, cant. of Dol. Pop. 2,076.
EPIRUS, a district of ancient Greece, now comprised in the Turkish prov. of Lower Albania.
EPISCOPIA, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Basilicata, district and 21 m. E of Lagonegro, on the slope of a hill, at the foot of which runs the Sinno. Pop. 1,500.

EPOISSES, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of the Côte-d'Or, cant. and 9 m. W of Semur. Pop. 1,006. It has an old castle, and possesses an extensive agricultural and horticultural establishment. Corn and cheese, the produce of the surrounding district, form its chief articles of trade.
EPONE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Seine-et-Oise, cant. of Mantes. Pop. 906.
EPPEGHEM, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of South Brabant, 4 m. NE of Vilvorde, on the l. bank of the Seine. Pop. 1,001.
EPPELHEIM, a village of Baden, in the circle of the Lower Rhine, to the W of Heidelberg. Pop. 800.

EPPENDORF, a town of Saxony, in the circle of the Erzgebirge, to the SE of Chemnitz. Pop. 1,515. -Also a town in the territory and 4 m. N of Hamburg, on the r. bank of the Alster. It has an asylum for the deaf and dumb, founded by Heineke in 1770. Pop. 1,350.
EPPERSTONE, a parish of Nottinghamshire, 4½ m. SW of Southwell, on a branch of the Trent. Area 2,280 acres. Pop. 518.

EPPING, a market-town and parish of Essex. The p. lies on the borders of Epping-forest. Area 5,250 acres. Pop. in 1801, 1,779; in 1831, with Epping-Upland, 2,440; in 1841, 2,424.—The town, 16 m. NE by N of London, was originally a hamlet of Waltham-abbey. It consists of one long and wide street, on a ridge of hills rising to about 380 ft. It is well supplied with water from land springs; and in the summer-months is much resorted to from the metropolis on account of its healthy and pleasant situation. It has long been celebrated for its royal chase, extending from E. to the vicinity of London, and formerly including a great part of the co. of Essex. A little to the SE, and divided from Eppingforest by the river Roding, is the forest of Hainault, where the venerable Fairlop-oak flourished for many centuries, its branches extending over a space of nearly 300 ft. in diam. This forest was in former ages a favourite resort of the citizens of London, for the enjoyment of the pleasures of archery; and up to the middle of last century it was a noted resort for gipsies; but the rapid increase of buildings and traffic has long broken up their haunts. It is still, however, the scene of an annual festival on Easter-Monday, when a stag is turned out to be hunted for the amusement of the civic sportsmen. The town of E. and the surrounding district are famous for the richness of their cream and butter, the EPT latter of which is nearly all consumed in London. E. sausages and pork are also a considerable article of consumption in London.
EPPING, a township of Rockingham co., in the state of New Hampshire, 30 m. ESE of Concord, watered by Lamprey and North rivers, and possessing a generally fertile soil. Pop. in 1840, 1,235.

EPPINGEN, a bailiwick and town of Baden, in the circle of the Middle Rhine, and on the Elsenz, 24 m. ENE of Carlsruhe. Pop. 3,237, of whom 622 are Catholics, and 222 Jews. It has considerable manufactories of linen. Pop. of bail. 15,657.
EPPING-UPLAND, a chapelry in the p. of Epping, Essex. Pop. 365.
EPPINSTEIN, or EPPENSTEIN, a village of Austria, in the prov. of Styria, circle of Judenburg. It has extensive iron and steel-works.
EPPISHAUSEN, a village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Thurgau, to the NE of Sulgen. Pop. 105.
EPPLEBY, a township in the p. of Forcett, N. R. of Yorkshire. Area 1,490 acres. Pop. 205.

EPPLETON (GREAT and LITTLE), townships in the p. of Houghton-le-Spring, co. of Durham. The former is 7½ m. NE of Durham, and is intersected by the Durham and Sunderland railway. Area of former 790 acres. Pop. 74. Area of latter 170 acres. Pop. 38.
EPPRAVE, a village of Belgium, in the prov. of Namur, to the W of Rochefort. Pop. 329.
EPPRECHSTEIN, a mountain of Bavaria, in the circle of Upper Franconia, near Kirchenlamitz. Alt. 2,741 ft.

EPSOM, a parish, formerly a market-town, in Surrey, 16 m. ENE of Guildford. Area 3,970 acres. Pop. in 1801, 2,404; in 1831, 3,231; in 1841, 3,533. In 1618, a medicinal spa, which issues from a hillock near Ashted, to the SE of the town, was discovered. Its properties are aperient; and sulphate of magnesia still retains the appellation of Epsom salts, from having been at one time to a great extent prepared from the waters of this spring. E. was long noted as a watering-place after the discovery of this spa; but, like other places of fashionable resort, it gradually became neglected and deserted. Another source of attraction, however, has been the more recent institution of annual races on the downs. These commence on Tuesday, and continue till the end of the week preceding Whitsuntide. They never fail to be numerously and fashionably attended. During the race-week, it has been calculated that E. is visited by from 300,000 to 400,000 persons. A grand stand was erected in 1829, at a great expense, for the accommodation of the company. It is 156 ft. in width, including the terrace, and 60 ft. in depth, and can accommodate nearly 5,000 persons, of whom 2,000 can find standing-room on the roof. A railway now unites E. with Croydon, and with London through the Croydon railway.
EPSOM, a township of Merrimac co., in the state of New Hampshire, U. S., 12 m. E of Concord. Its surface is irregular, and is watered by Inncook river. Pop. in 1840, 1,205.
EPSTEIN, a town of the duchy of Nassau, to the NE of Wiesbaden, on the l. bank of the Guldenbach. Pop. 1,200. It has a church, and the remains of a castle.

EPTE, a river of France, which has its source in the dep. of the Seine-Inferieure, 2 m. N of Forges; flows near Gournay; separates to a considerable extent the dep. of the Eure from those of the Oise and the Seine-et-Oise; passes Gisors and St. Clair; and, after a total course in a generally S direction of 51 m., falls into the Seine, by two branches, about 2 m. above Vernon.
EPTERODE, a town of Hesse-Cassel, in the prov. of the Nieder-Hessen, circle of Witzerhausen, near Gross-Almerode. Pop. 352. It is noted for its manufacture of crucibles.
EPTINGEN, a village of Switzerland, in the cant. and 17 m. SE of Basle, district of Waldenburg. Pop. 646. It is noted for its mineral springs and baths.
EPWELL, a chapelry in the p. of Swalcliffe, Oxfordshire. Area 1,270 acres. Pop. 316.
EQUAN, a river of British North America, which issues from a lake of the same name, in the district of Albany, and falls into James bay to the S of Equan point.
EQUATOR. See ECUADOR.
EQUENOY, a village of France, in the dep. of the Oise, cant. and 2 m. NW of Breteuil.
EQUEURDREVILLE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Manche, cant. of Octeville. Pop. 1,609.
EQUI, a village of Tuscany, in the campanato of Pisa, 8 m. SE of Tivizzano. Pop. 159. Fine white marble is quarried in the environs.
EQUINABIS, a tribe of aborigines who inhabit the banks of some of the head-streams of the Atabapo, on the confines of Spanish Guayana, and New Grenada, in 3° N lat.
ER (Kis), or Klein-Er, a town of Hungary, in the comitat of Jazygie. Pop. 5,302.

ERA, a river of Tuscany, in the prov. of Pisa, which descends from a branch of the Apennines to the E of Volterra; traverses a valley of the same name; and falls into the Arno, on the l. bank, near Ponte d'Era, after a course, in a generally NW direction, of 27 m.
ERABU, an island of the N. Pacific, in the group of the Majicosimah islands, to the E of the island of Typinsan, in N lat. 24° 48', E long. 124° 40'.
ERAKLI. See EREKLI.
ERAMO (Santo), a town of Naples, in the prov. of the Terra-di-Bari, district and 12 m. ESE of Altamura.
ERAMOSA, a township of Upper Canada, in the Wellington district, intersected by a branch of the Grand river. The soil of the upper portion of the township is excellent. Pop. in 1841, 935.
ERAMRA, or Aramraw, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Gujerat, at the NW extremity of the peninsula of that name, on the gulf of Cutch.
ERAVINSK, a town of Asiatic Russia, in the gov. and 310 m. ENE of Irkutsk, near the source of the river Vitim.
ERBA, a district and village of Venetian Lombardy, in the prov. and 8 m. E of Como, and on the road from that town to Bergamo. Pop. 800.—The district comprises 28 com. Pop. 10,744.

ERBACH, a river of Bavaria, in the circle of the Pfalz, formed by two streams, the Fischbach and the Schwartzbach, which descend from the W side of the Vosges, and unite near Wald-fischbach; thence it pursues a circuitous course past Contwig and Deux- Ponts, receives the Hornbach, and throws itself into the Blies near Ingweiler. Its total course does not exceed 24 m.—Also a grafschaft and town of the grand duchy of Hesse-Darmstadt, prov. of Starkenburg. The town is 23 m. SE of Darmstadt, and 29 m. NE of Mannheim, on the Mümling. Pop. 2,078. It has a ducal castle, built in 1736, in which is the famous coffer of Eginhard, and other antiquities. It possesses manufactories of cloth and fire-arms, several tanneries, and a printing establishment. The graf. has an area of 250 sq. m. Pop. of the graf. 37,600.—Also a village of Nassau, in the Rheingau, 1 m. W of Eltville, and 8 m. W of Mayence, on the r. bank of the Rhine. Pop. 1,150. The environs are noted for producing the best of the Rhenish wines .- Also a town of Wurtemberg, in the circle of the Danube, bail. and 10 m. ENE of Ehingen, at an alt. ERC of 1,726 ft. above sea-level. Pop. (Cath.) in 1840, 1,067.
